text: The surgeon attempts to decompress the gallbladder using a large needle, with minimal bile visibly being extracted from the organ.
A large stone is visibly lodged at the bottom of the gallbladder, presenting a challenging aspect of the case.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum on both sides at the bottom of the gallbladder and begins inspecting the Calot's triangle for anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies a lateral branch of the artery near the gallbladder, noting its presence without concern for significant bleeding or complications.
The surgeon continues to open the peritoneum around the bottom of the gallbladder, attempting to lift it upwards and out of its position.
The surgeon attempts to open the peritoneum along the lower part of the gallbladder, aiming to improve visibility and facilitate lifting the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes the continuation of the dissection path downwards, focusing on the area around the gallbladder.
The surgeon attempts to create space around the gallbladder to increase its mobility for manipulation, using careful dissection techniques.

The surgeon attempts to access the medial side of the gallbladder, working to get behind it using a modified top-down approach to better expose the bottom of the gallbladder. The surgeon maneuvers back and forth to achieve this exposure.

The surgeon views the surgical plane from a new angle behind the gallbladder, encountering a large stone at the bottom that obstructs manipulation. The surgeon attempts to approach from the opposite side to improve access.
The surgeon pauses to reassess the anatomical layout, cleaning the surgical field. A small bleeder is identified and requires attention.
The surgeon identifies a structure that appears to be the cystic artery, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon maneuvers around the gallbladder, preparing to divide the tissue, with visible manipulation to expose the area for further dissection.

The surgeon attempts to maneuver behind the gallbladder, palpating for the large stone beneath. The gallbladder is visibly extended, and the surgeon tries to push the stone upward to improve retraction, but the stone remains immobile.
The surgeon continues to work upwards along the gallbladder, carefully maneuvering around the anatomical structures.
The surgeon struggles to maneuver both instruments into the angle, creating a small hole in the gallbladder to suction out residual bile, though it does not significantly decompress the gallbladder.
The surgeon maneuvers behind the lip of the gallbladder to ensure complete removal of the gallbladder wall, carefully navigating around the structure identified as the artery.
The surgeon continues to manipulate the gallbladder, working back and forth to separate tissue from known areas to unknown areas, focusing on easier sections for dissection.
The surgeon attempts to maneuver behind the gallbladder, deciding to employ a top-down approach to better visualize and manipulate the stone within the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a sponge to push against the liver for retraction, utilizing the hole made in the gallbladder as a buttress to prevent instruments from penetrating the liver parenchyma.
The surgeon has improved visibility and is in the process of freeing the top three quarters of the gallbladder from the liver bed, allowing a clear view behind the gallbladder.

The surgeon navigates through the lumen of the gallbladder, examining the interior from the previously created hole, while aiming to locate the duct. The stone remains immovable further down within the gallbladder.
The surgeon widens the opening in the gallbladder to access a large stone visible within the lumen, preparing to extract it.
The surgeon widens the opening in the gallbladder to extract the stone, which becomes visible as it is maneuvered out.

With the stone removed, the surgeon views the interior of the gallbladder lumen, assessing the remaining gallbladder tissue below. The surgeon then grasps and manipulates the gallbladder for further dissection.
The surgeon continues to work from the posterior aspect of the gallbladder, attempting to separate the peritoneum, which is resistant to tearing, using a back-and-forth motion.
The surgeon amputates the majority of the gallbladder, aiming to facilitate further manipulation and removal of the remaining tissue.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder, revealing the bottom portion of the organ, allowing for further manipulation and visualization of the remaining tissue.
The surgeon identifies a structure resembling the cystic duct, which appears to be the only remaining attachment to the liver. The surgeon clears surrounding tissue to confirm its identity.

The gallbladder is pressed against the common bile duct, potentially causing a Mirizzi syndrome. The surgeon identifies the area resembling the Calot's triangle and begins to develop this anatomical space.
The surgeon peels down the artery, revealing the back wall of the gallbladder that has been pursued throughout the procedure.
The surgeon visually inspects the lumen of the duct, clearly identifying it as the cystic duct, which is the remaining structure attached to the liver.
